article:
Chunk # 0.
Rapid isolation of respiring
skeletal muscle mitochondria
using nitrogen cavitation
Awais Z Younis, Gareth G Lavery, Mark Christian and
Craig L Doig*
Department of Biosciences, School of Science and Technology, Nottingham Trent University,
Nottingham, United Kingdom
Methods of isolating mitochondria co mmonly utilise mechanical force and
shear stress to homogenize tissue followed by puri ﬁcation by multiple rounds
of ultracentrifugation Existing proto cols can be time-consuming with some
physically impairing integrity of the se nsitive mitochondria l double membrane Here, we describe a method for the recovery of intact, respiring mitochondriafrom murine skeletal muscle tissue and cell lines using nitrogen cavitation This
protocol results in high-yield, pure and respiring mitochondria without the
need for puri ﬁcation gradients or ultracentrifugation The protocol takes under
an hour and requires limited specialised equipment Our methodology issuccessful in extracting mitochondr ia of both cell extracts and skeletal
muscle tissue This represents an improved yield in comparison to many of
the existing methods Western blotting and electron microscopy demonstrate
the enrichment of mitochondria with the ir ultrastructure well-preserved and
an absence of contamination from cytoplasmic or nuclear fractions Usingrespirometry analysis we show that mitochondria extracted from murineskeletal muscle cell lines (C2C12) and tibialis anterior tissue have an
appropriate respiratory control ratio These measures are indicative ofhealthy coupled mitochondria Our me thod successfully demonstrates the
rapid isolation of functional mitochondria and will bene ﬁt researchers studying
mitochondrial bioenergetics as well as providing greater throughput and
application for time-sensitive assays KEYWORDS
mitochondria, cavitation, skeletal muscle, protocol, methodolody
Highlights
Pure, respiring mitochondria in under an hour without complex puri ﬁcation
gradients Effective mitochondrial isolation from skeletal muscle cells and tissue Potential use to those working with in vivo primary endpoints that have downstream
in vitro mitochondrial evaluation.OPEN ACCESS
EDITED BY
Antentor Hinton,
Vanderbilt University, United States
REVIEWED BY
John Hollander,West Virginia University, United StatesCarol Witczak,Indiana University Bloomington,
United States
*CORRESPONDENCE
Craig L Doig,
craig.doig@ntu.ac.uk

Mitochondrial Research,a section of the journalFrontiers in Physiology

PUBLISHED 07 March 2023

using nitrogen cavitation.Front Physiol 14:1114595 doi: 10.3389/fphys.2023.1114595

cyanide-p-tri ﬂuoromethoxyphenylhydrazone; TBS, Tris-buffered saline; RCR, Respiratory control ratio Frontiers in Physiology frontiersin.org 01TYPE Methods
PUBLISHED 07 March 2023
DOI10.3389/fphys.2023.1114595Introduction
Required for locomotion, respiration, and storage of nutrients,
skeletal muscle depots are densely packed with mitochondria Assuch, optimal mitochondrial function is fundamental to bothcellular behaviour and preservation of whole-body energy
homeostasis As appreciation of tissue-speci ﬁc mitochondrial biology advances,
there is a growing need to develop alternative methodologicalapproaches to rapidly isolate and e valuate mitochondrial phenotype However, to examine respiratory function, isolation from whole-cellconstituents must be both ef ﬁcient and robust Currently, this can be
achieved using a variety of techniques such as differential centrifugation(Drissi et al., 2013 ), afﬁnity puri ﬁcation ( Dunham et al., 2012 )a n df r e e -
ﬂow electrophoresis/ ﬁeld-ﬂow fractionation ( Islinger et al., 2010 ) However, there are limitations to these methods including reducedpurity and yield Moreover, certain procedures require costly reagents(Sims and Anderson, 2008 ;Franko et al., 2013 ) Reproducible lysis of
cellular membranes between samples is also important, particularlygiven the resilient nature of skeletal muscle cells ( Franzini-Armstrong
and Engel, 2012 ;Liao et al., 2020 ) Commonly, this is resolved through
mechanical homogenisation or the application of shear stress Whilst
these are effective, they can compromise organelle integrity and produce
compartment leakage This is evidenced by a recent review ( Liao et al.,
2020) suggesting that mitochondria i solated using homogenisation
show reduced integrity Distinct methods of mitochondrial isolationalso have speci ﬁc requirements of time, technical capability, and
specialist equipment, variables t hat contribute to differences in
recovered purity and yield An ideal method should provide time-ef ﬁcient mitochondrial
isolation free from major contaminants and produce reproducible
Respiratory Control Ratio (RCR) values consistent with anestablished understanding of mitochondrial function Here weapply nitrogen cavitation to cultured skeletal muscle cells andmurine tissue, a procedure in which nitrogen gas is diffused intocells under pressure and when released causes lysis of cellmembranes for subsequent mitochondrial isolation ( Gottlieb and
Adachi, 2000 ) This coupled with a rapid differential centrifugation
process allowed recovery of mitochondria in high yield, purity and
suitable for high-resolution respiratory studies
summary:
SPR:
- Rapid isolation of respiring skeletal muscle mitochondria using nitrogen cavitation.
- Method eliminates the need for purification gradients or ultracentrifugation.
- High-yield, pure mitochondria obtained in under an hour.
- Suitable for both cell extracts and skeletal muscle tissue.
- Mitochondrial integrity preserved, free from contamination.
- Appropriate respiratory control ratio indicates healthy mitochondria.
- Beneficial for researchers studying mitochondrial bioenergetics.

summary: Revised Summary: The protocol involves isolating mitochondria from tissue and cells with a low variation in Respiratory Control Ratio (RCR) of ±0.2 and 0.8 SEM, respectively. Mice (B6j) from Charles River in the United Kingdom were group housed in controlled conditions with a 12-hour light-dark cycle and had ad libitum access to chow and water. Skeletal muscle tissue (tibialis anterior) was dissected and placed in ice-cold sucrose buffer (10 mM HEPES, pH 7.5, 70 mM sucrose, 200 mM mannitol, 1 mM EGTA, 1% protease inhibitors, and 1% phosphatase inhibitors). C2C12 murine myoblasts (ATCC) were maintained in high glucose-Dulbecco's Minimal Eagle's Medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% (v/v) fetal bovine serum, 1% L-glutamine (v/v), and 1% penicillin-streptomycin (v/v). Cells were used at 80% confluence between passages 18-28. Cell pellets were collected by washing the monolayer with DPBS- and incubating with trypsin. The tissue and cell extracts were subjected to the same steps, including nitrogen cavitation and centrifugation, to isolate the mitochondria. The resulting mitochondrial pellets were resuspended in mitochondrial assay buffer (MAS) (70 mM sucrose, 210 mM mannitol, 2 mM HEPES, 1 mM EGTA, 5 mM magnesium chloride, 10 mM potassium dihydrogen phosphate, 0.2% fatty acid-free bovine serum albumin, 10 mM sodium pyruvate, 2 mM D-Malic acid). Recovered mitochondria can be visualized. The study emphasizes the suitability of this protocol for studying mitochondria in skeletal muscle for high-resolution respiratory studies and proteome analysis. All animal experiments were conducted in accordance with United Kingdom Home Office regulations. Immunoblotting was performed using total homogenate. The full method for mitochondrial isolation using mechanical homogenization-based methods is provided by Garcia and colleagues (Garcia-Cazarin et al.). The protocol is relevant for researchers interested in mitochondrial isolation, respirometry, proteome analysis, cell culture, myocyte differentiation, and nitrogen cavitation.

summary: The supernatant was transferred to a fresh tube and centrifuged at 10,500 g for 10 minutes. The resulting pellet was resuspended in 500 µL of isolation buffer 2 and the supernatant was collected as the 'cyto' fraction. The pellet was centrifuged again at 10,500 g for 10 minutes, resulting in the mitochondrial fraction, which was resuspended in 100 µL of isolation buffer 2. The mitochondrial fraction was quantified using the mitochondrial isolation kit for cells from Thermo Fisher. The mitochondria were extracted using nitrogen cavitation and differential centrifugation. Mitochondrial respiratory studies were performed using the SeahorseXF24 sensor cartridge. Western blot analysis was conducted using various antibodies. Protein lysates were quantified using the Bio-Rad DC protein assay kit. Equal protein aliquots were separated using sodium dodecyl sulfate-polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis. The separated proteins were transferred onto a nitrocellulose membrane. Antibodies were used for primary and secondary immunoblotting. Transmission electron microscopy was used to examine the samples.

summary:
Iteration 6:
- Mitochondria are isolated from a cell pellet using a mechanical homogenisation-based method and a nitrogen cavitation-based method.
- The nitrogen cavitation method successfully isolates nearly 30% of the cell's mitochondria in 45 minutes.
- Mitochondria make up between 3% - 5% of skeletal muscle.
- The mechanical homogenisation-based method results in contaminated mitochondrial samples with nuclear (histone H3) and cytosolic (GAPDH) proteins.
- The nitrogen cavitation method produces lower variability and high reproducibility compared to the mechanical homogenisation-based method.
- Nitrogen cavitation is a method for isolating mitochondria from cells and tissues.
- The method preserves the structural integrity and functional capacity of mitochondria.
- Nitrogen cavitation produces a high yield of pure mitochondrial protein (830 µg from a 330 mg cell pellet and 358 µg from an average tissue weight of 107 mg).
- The method shows low variability and high reproducibility.
- Isolated mitochondria show no contamination from cytosolic or nuclear proteins and are enriched in the mitochondrial fraction, confirmed by Western blot analysis of cytochrome C and COX IV.
- The nitrogen cavitation method maintains mitochondrial respiratory activity, as demonstrated by the response to complex I, III, and V inhibitors.
- The respiratory control ratio of isolated mitochondria from C2C12 myoblast cells is 4.9 ± 0.8 in the presence of malate and glutamate, and for skeletal muscle tissue is 4.6 ± 0.2.
- Mechanical homogenization-based methods may disrupt the structural integrity of mitochondria, reducing their functional capacity.
- Mechanical homogenization-based methods result in a lower total yield of mitochondria (104 µg from an initial tissue weight of 166 mg) compared to nitrogen cavitation (0.1% recovery compared to 0.34%).
- Comparison of nitrogen cavitation and mechanical homogenization shows lower variability and higher reproducibility with nitrogen cavitation.
- Nitrogen cavitation isolates mitochondria without contamination from cytosolic or nuclear proteins.
- Western blot analysis confirms enrichment of the mitochondrial fraction using nitrogen cavitation.

summary: Summary 7: A new protocol for mitochondrial isolation was proposed, considering the demands of the experimental workflow and downstream endpoints. The protocol allows for rapid and robust isolation of mitochondria, making it suitable for larger-scale studies and high-throughput respirometry analysis. The study compared different methodologies, highlighting variations in cell type, reagents, equipment, and methods. The protocol ensures the isolation of intact, contaminant-free, respiring mitochondria with a comparable respiratory control ratio. Although some endoplasmic reticulum contamination was observed, there were no nuclear, cytoplasmic, or whole-cell contaminants. The protocol offers advantages for mitochondrial proteomic analysis and is applicable to skeletal muscle tissue and cells. The study utilized electron microscopy data from the Imaging Suite at Nottingham Trent University and acknowledged funding from Nottingham Trent University Quality Research Funding. Supplementary material, including Western blot analysis and mitochondrial recovery data, is available online.

topics.topic: Intact and contaminant-free mitochondria
topics.justification: The protocol ensures the isolation of intact, contaminant-free, respiring mitochondria with a comparable respiratory control ratio. It mentions the absence of nuclear, cytoplasmic, or whole-cell contaminants, although some endoplasmic reticulum contamination was observed.

summary: Revised Summary: Mitochondrial respiration rates were measured in muscle cells and muscle tissue, and the respiratory control ratio (State III and State IV) was calculated. A mechanical homogenization-based method can isolate muscle tissue and yield a high amount of mitochondrial proteins. The input pellet weight and the recovered mitochondrial pellet are crucial factors in the isolation process. "Frontiers in Physiology" is a journal that publishes research on muscle cells and mitochondrial function. Researchers like Djafarzadeh, Drissi, Dubois, and Boisvert have contributed to the field. Proteomics methods, including mass spectrometry, can analyze the mitochondrial proteome. Tissue disruption and enrichment with anti-TOM22 magnetic beads efficiently isolate pure and functional mitochondria from mouse tissues. The interface between the endoplasmic reticulum (ER) and mitochondria plays a critical role in cellular functions. Mitochondria are involved in aging, age-associated diseases, and cardiac health. Techniques like nitrogen cavitation and superparamagnetic microbeads can isolate mitochondria from cells and tissues. Mitochondria-endoplasmic reticulum contacts are important for cellular communication and have implications in cancer. The Seahorse XFe24 analyzer is commonly used to measure respiratory function in isolated cardiac mitochondria. Percoll density gradient centrifugation can isolate mitochondria from rat brain. Catecholamines can affect mitochondrial respiration in hepatic and skeletal muscle cells, especially in the context of faecal peritonitis. Communication between mitochondria and other organelles is a new perspective in understanding the role of mitochondria in cancer.
